examples.title: Linear Search Algorithm
examples.description: Sequential search through array to find target element
examples.code:
LINEAR_SEARCH(arr, n, key)
 FOR i FROM 0 TO n-1 DO
 IF arr[i] == key THEN
 RETURN i
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN -1
examples.explanation: Iterates through each element sequentially until match is found or array ends. Returns index if found, -1 otherwise.

examples.title: Binary Search Algorithm
examples.description: Efficient search on sorted array using divide and conquer
examples.code:
BINARY_SEARCH(arr, low, high, key)
 WHILE low <= high DO
 mid = (low + high) / 2
 IF arr[mid] == key THEN
 RETURN mid
 ELSE IF arr[mid] < key THEN
 low = mid + 1
 ELSE
 high = mid - 1
 END IF
 END WHILE
 RETURN -1
examples.explanation: Repeatedly divides search space in half by comparing middle element with target.

examples.title: Recursive Factorial Calculation
examples.description: Factorial using recursion demonstrating base case
examples.code:
FACTORIAL(n)
 IF n <= 1 THEN
 RETURN 1 // Base case
 ELSE
 RETURN n * FACTORIAL(n-1) // Recursive call
 END IF
examples.explanation: Base case stops recursion at n=1. Each call multiplies current n with factorial of (n-1).

examples.title: Bubble Sort Algorithm
examples.description: Simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly swaps adjacent elements
examples.code:
BUBBLE_SORT(arr, n)
 FOR i FROM 0 TO n-2 DO
 FOR j FROM 0 TO n-i-2 DO
 IF arr[j] > arr[j+1] THEN
 SWAP(arr[j], arr[j+1]) // Swap if in wrong order
 END IF
 END FOR
 END FOR
examples.explanation: Larger elements bubble up to the end. After each pass, largest unsorted element is placed in correct position.

examples.title: Merge Sort Algorithm
examples.code:
MERGE_SORT(arr, left, right)
 IF left < right THEN
 mid = (left + right) / 2
 MERGE_SORT(arr, left, mid)
 MERGE_SORT(arr, mid+1, right)
 MERGE(arr, left, mid, right)
 END IF

MERGE(arr, l, m, r)
 // Merge two sorted subarrays into one sorted array
examples.explanation: Divides array into halves, recursively sorts them, then merges sorted halves.

examples.title: Stack Operations
examples.description: Implementation of basic stack operations using array
examples.code:
// Push operation
PUSH(stack, top, size, element)
 IF top >= size - 1 THEN
 RETURN "Stack Overflow"
 ELSE
 top = top + 1
 stack[top] = element
 END IF

// Pop operation
POP(stack, top)
 IF top < 0 THEN
 RETURN "Stack Underflow"
 ELSE
 element = stack[top]
 top = top - 1
 RETURN element
 END IF
examples.explanation: Push adds element at top, Pop removes element from top. Both check for overflow/underflow conditions.

examples.title: Queue Operations
examples.description: Implementation of basic queue operations using array
examples.code:
// Enqueue operation
ENQUEUE(queue, rear, size, element)
 IF rear >= size - 1 THEN
 RETURN "Queue Full"
 ELSE
 rear = rear + 1
 queue[rear] = element
 END IF

// Dequeue operation
DEQUEUE(queue, front, rear)
 IF front > rear THEN
 RETURN "Queue Empty"
 ELSE
 element = queue[front]
 front = front + 1
 RETURN element
 END IF
examples.explanation: Enqueue adds at rear, Dequeue removes from front. Uses FIFO principle.

examples.title: Dynamic Programming - Fibonacci
examples.description: Fibonacci using memoization to optimize recursion
examples.code:
FIB_MEMO(n, memo)
 IF n <= 1 THEN
 RETURN n
 END IF
 IF memo[n] != -1 THEN
 RETURN memo[n]
 END IF
 memo[n] = FIB_MEMO(n-1, memo) + FIB_MEMO(n-2, memo)
 RETURN memo[n]

// Initialize memo array with -1
// Call: FIB_MEMO(n, memo)
examples.explanation: Stores computed Fibonacci values in memo array. Avoids recalculating same values, reducing from O(2^n) to O(n).
